secsa__001_a.text:
Health inequalities persist in the diagnosis, treatment, and outcomes of inflammatory bowel disease
(IBD) among ethnic minority populations, particularly within South Asian (SA) communities in the UK.
This multi-phase, mixed-methods study aims to examine these disparities and inform the proposal of
culturally tailored interventions to improve IBD care for SA populations.
Phase 1: a retrospective cohort analysis of routinely collected healthcare data from the Clinical Practice
Research Datalink (CPRD) linked with Hospital Episode Statistics (HES), Office for National Statistics
(ONS), and the Index of Multiple Deprivation (IMD). The study will identify a cohort of SA patients with
IBD diagnosed between 2005 and 2025 and compare their healthcare outcomes with non-SA patients.
Key outcomes include time to diagnosis, treatment initiation, hospital admissions, surgical interventions,
and mortality. Statistical analyses, including Cox regression and Poisson models, will adjust for
confounding factors such as age, sex, comorbidities, and socioeconomic status. Phase 1 findings will
inform Phase 2 by identifying key care disparities, guiding in-depth exploration of barriers and facilitators

secsa__001_b.text:
to effective IBD management.
Phase 2: expanding upon findings from Phase 1, through qualitative research using interpretive
phenomenology. Semi-structured interviews will be conducted with 30 - 40 participants, including SA
patients with IBD and healthcare professionals (HCPs) involved in IBD care. Participants will be
recruited via charities such as Crohn’s & Colitis UK (CCUK) and the South Asian IBD Alliance (SAIA).
Interviews will involve reflexive, inductive analysis identifying thematic insights into how healthcare
experiences are shaped by ethnicity, and the meaning this has for participants, exploring aspects
including but not limited to lived experiences, cultural barriers, and structural inequalities in accessing
care. Findings from this phase will provide rich information on IBD and will inform the development of
Phase 3.
Phase 3: a realist review to explore how, why, for whom, and in what contexts interventions aimed at
reducing health inequalities in diseases commonly affecting SA populations, specifically cardiovascular
disease (CVD), chronic kidney disease (CKD), diabetes mellitus (DM), and mental health (MH). Using
the RAMESES framework, the review will follow five

secsa__001_c.text:
iterative stages: developing initial programme
theories, systematically searching and selecting relevant evidence, extracting data to identify context-
mechanism-outcome configurations (CMOCs), and refining these theories through interpretive analysis
and stakeholder engagement. The review will include peer-reviewed and grey literature from sources
such as MEDLINE, Embase, and NHS Evidence. The outcome will include a set of actionable, theory-
informed recommendations and transferable strategies relevant to IBD care aligned with national health
equity policies such as the NHS Long Term Plan and CORE20PLUS5.
Collectively, these phases will provide a comprehensive understanding of ethnic disparities in IBD care
from both quantitative and qualitative perspectives. The integration of large-scale data analysis, lived
experience, and evidence synthesis offers a robust foundation for proposing culturally appropriate,
evidence-based interventions. The study’s outputs aim to inform policy, enhance equity in healthcare
delivery, and ultimately improve outcomes for patients living with IBD.

secpesor__001_a.text:
The research fellowship aims to understand how culture, language, and access to healthcare affect
South Asian people with inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), a group of conditions including Crohn’s
disease and ulcerative colitis. These are long-term conditions with no cure, and they can cause
symptoms like diarrhoea, stomach pain, fatigue, and weight loss. IBD affects both daily life and
specifically quality of life. It is becoming more prevalent globally, and the UK has one of the highest rates
of IBD in the world.
People from South Asian backgrounds are more likely to be affected by IBD in the UK. These
communities are growing and will make up a large part of the UK population in the future. Language
barriers, cultural differences, and lack of awareness about the condition play a role in South Asians often
experiencing worse health outcomes. By listening to patients and working with community groups, the
research aims to improve care, raise awareness, and reduce health inequalities in the long term through
3 phases of research:
Phase 1 of the research aims to understand how care pathways and IBD outcomes for South Asian
people compare with general populations based on feedback from
secpesor__001_a.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_b.text:
research and the patient engagement
team. Using existing NHS health records from GP practices and hospitals, the study will examine the
experiences of people diagnosed with IBD, focusing in particular on those of South Asian heritage. The
research will explore how long it takes South Asian patients to be diagnosed and start treatment, how
often they are admitted to hospital or need surgery, and how frequently they use healthcare services
such as GP visits or A&E. The findings will provide important evidence about health inequalities in IBD
care and will help shape the next stage of the project.
Phase 2 of the project will explore South Asian people’s experiences with IBD treatment and treatment
burden as discovered in Phase 1. Both patients and healthcare professionals will be interviewed to
understand personal stories and professional insights around IBD care. The aim is to gain a deeper
understanding of the challenges South Asian patients face and how healthcare services can better
support them. The interviews will be carefully analysed to highlight key themes that may explain health
inequalities and guide future improvements.
Phase 3 of the project, a realist review, will look
secpesor__001_b.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secpesor__001_c.text:
at how and why, certain healthcare programmes do or
do not work for South Asian people living with health conditions like heart disease, kidney disease,
diabetes, and mental health conditions. It will focus on understanding what makes these programmes
successful, who benefits most from them, and what factors help or could be improved. The study will
follow a structured approach working closely with patients, healthcare workers, and community
members to make sure the findings are meaningful and useful. The goal is to use what is learnt to help
design better care for South Asian people with IBD.
This 3-stage research is important to understand how current healthcare services for IBD affect how
patients are cared for. Findings from the research will recommend how services can be improved for
people living with IBD.
secpesor__001_c.source_path: APPLICATION DETAILS > Plain English Summary of Research

secppi__001_a.text:
The integration of Patient and Public Involvement and Engagement (PPIE) throughout the project
lifecycle will be fundamental to ensuring this study reflects the lived realities, needs, and priorities of
South Asian (SA) communities affected by Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD). PPIE will not be confined
to individual phases, but will serve as a cross-cutting, embedded approach from inception to
dissemination, in line with the NIHR’s UK Standards for Public Involvement, focusing on inclusive
opportunities, working together, support and learning, governance, communications, and impact. All
PPIE contributors will be reimbursed in line with NIHR INVOLVE guidance, and their contributions
formally recognised in all dissemination activities and publications.
This group will include SA patients with IBD (spanning age, gender, religion, and language), carers and
family members, and local community leaders or advocates. The group will meet virtually, quarterly to
co-develop research materials, help shape the interpretation of findings, co-author outputs, and
participate in project management. All PPIE contributors will be offered tailored training in the research
process, including qualitativ

secppi__001_b.text:
e and quantitative methods, data analysis, ethical considerations, and
dissemination strategies. They will also receive support to understand IBD and the UK healthcare
system. Reflective practices, such as journaling and debrief sessions, will be encouraged to support
learning and evaluate their involvement experience.
PPIE members will offer real-time insights into community-specific health beliefs and practices. These
perspectives will shape recruitment approaches, the design of interviews, and interpretation of both
quantitative and qualitative data, directly influencing the proposal of interventions in later project phases.
All communications with PPIE contributors will be designed to be culturally sensitive and accessible.
Materials including participant information sheets, consent forms, and visual aids will be produced in
plain English and translated into relevant languages. These will be tested with PPIE members for clarity
and tone. Communication methods will include multilingual webinars, podcasts, in-person gatherings,
and community-led events, aligning with how information is most effectively shared within SA
communities.
Dissemination activities will be co-designed wit

secppi__001_c.text:
h the PPIE group to ensure findings are shared in formats
that are both accessible and academically rigorous. These will include community workshops,
infographics, bilingual videos, and co-authored academic publications. PPIE contributors will be
supported to co-present at conferences, attend stakeholder meetings, and co-deliver public seminars,
fostering a strong sense of ownership and advocacy.
Finally, the impact of PPIE will be evaluated throughout the project using feedback forms, reflective
interviews, and researcher reflections to assess how involvement has influenced the study. This
commitment to continuous improvement will provide evidence of PPIE’s value, methodologically,
culturally, and ethically. By embedding PPIE as a foundational pillar, this project aims to be inclusive,
culturally responsive, and impactful, creating lasting trust and collaboration between academic and
community partners.

secsb__001_a.text: Salary Costs £187,235.95 The lead applicant will be supported at 100% FTE for 3 years, based on current academic pay scales at King’s College London (KCL), in line with applicant's current salary, with appropriate annual increments factored in. Advanced Epidemiological Analysis, 11 days, £2,950, deepens expertise in longitudinal and multivariable methods for health inequalities. University College London (UCL) Practical Statistics for Medical Research, 4 days, £550, application of biostatistical techniques Qualitative: £5,068 University of Oxford Advanced Qualitative Research Methods, 5 days, £3,390, training for handling complex qualitative datasets. secac__001.text:
SUMMARY INFORMATION > Application Title:
Care pathways and outcomes in South Asians with Inflammatory Bowel Disease: a mixed methods study

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Contracting Organisation:
King's College London

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Start Date:
01/06/2026

SUMMARY INFORMATION > End Date:
31/05/2029

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Duration (months):
36

SUMMARY INFORMATION > Has this application been previously submitted to this or any other funding body?:
No

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Full Name:
Mr Harinder Sokhal

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Organisation:
King's College London

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > Department:
Florence Nightingale Faculty of Nursing, Midwifery & Palliative Care Professional Background Other Health or Care Practitioner Detailed Professional Pharmacist Background Professional Registration General Pharmaceutical Council, registration number: 2204256

LEAD APPLICANT & RESEARCH TEAM > Lead Applicant > ORCID:
0009-0006-3492-6735

SUPPORT AND MENTORSHIP:
Organisation King's College London Email Address alexandru.dregan@kcl.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support An experienced epidemiologist with extensive CPRD experience, Dr Provided Dregan will provide ongoing supervision and methodological support, including causal inference methods, longitudinal modeling, and data linkage analysis, with a focus in addressing health inequalities in ethnic minorities. Full Name Professor Jack Satsangi Organisation University of Oxford Email Address jack.satsangi@ndm.ox.ac.uk Role Mentor Description of Support Professor Jack Satsangi will provide senior academic mentorship and Provided clinical expertise to support my NIHR Doctoral Fellowship, drawing on his internationally recognised leadership in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) research and patient care. As the Lee Placito Professor of Gastroenterology at the University of Oxford and a longstanding advocate for translational research and patient-centred innovation, Professor Satsangi brings unparalleled experience in the genetics, phenotyping, and personalised treatment of IBD. His involvement in major national and international research consortia, alongside his advisory roles with Crohn’s & Colitis UK and the UK IBD Registry, ensures the project remains aligned with national research priorities and responsive to patient needs. His guidance will help shape a research programme that is methodologically rigorous, clinically relevant, and embedded within key NIHR frameworks for impact, capacity-building, and equitable care innovation. Full Name Dr Wladyslawa Czuber-Dochan Organisation King's College London Email Address wladzia.czuber-dochan@kcl.ac.uk Role Primary Academic Supervisor Description of Support Dr Wladyslawa Czuber-Dochan’s current research programme is highly Provided aligned with my NIHR application and will provide a strong foundation for his proposed doctoral work. Dr Czuber-Dochan’s research centres on the management of inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), which is also the primary focus of my PhD. The overarching aim of Wladyslawa’s research is to enhance symptom management and improve quality of life for individuals living with IBD. As a qualitative and mixed methods researcher, she has particular expertise in phenomenology, experience- based co-design, and the development and evaluation of complex interventions. A key strand of her work addresses the symptom burden experienced by individuals with IBD, with a particular focus on fatigue, pain, psychological distress, stress and resilience, and food-related quality of life. These areas are of particular relevance to pharmacists and allied health professionals involved in medication optimisation, behavioural intervention, culturally competent care, and the delivery of holistic, person-centred services. Dr Czuber-Dochan’s programme of work also considers the differential impact of IBD on underserved and ethnically diverse populations. She has a particular interest in addressing health inequalities among South Asian communities, who experience a rising incidence of IBD and may face specific challenges related to dietary management, symptom reporting, help-seeking behaviours, and access to culturally appropriate support. This population is often underrepresented in clinical research, and Wladyslawa is committed to ensuring that interventions are inclusive, co-designed with diverse participants, and evaluated for cultural relevance. Dr Czuber-Dochan has served as Principal Investigator or Co- Investigator on 16 externally funded research studies, several of which have contributed to the international understanding of fatigue in IBD. A key outcome from this body of work is the IBD-Fatigue Self-Assessment Scale, which she led the development of. This tool has been translated and validated in 20 countries and is now used in both research and clinical practice. Importantly, it has undergone robust cultural adaptation to ensure relevance across diverse ethnic and cultural groups. Dr Czuber-Dochan’s recent work includes the process evaluation and optimisation of complex, digital behavioural interventions for fatigue, pain, and faecal incontinence in IBD. The digital intervention aims to improve accessibility, and form part of an NIHR Programme Grant (£2.3M), on which she leads the process evaluation component. These interventions are designed to be implemented by members of the multidisciplinary team, offering an important opportunity for pharmacists and allied health professionals to contribute to culturally responsive care and support for self-management in diverse populations. Dr Czuber-Dochan was former Chair of the Scientific Advisory Board for Crohn’s & Colitis UK and former Chair of Nurse-ECCO. She brings extensive experience in research leadership and supervision, having previously served as Associate Dean for Postgraduate Research. Dr Czuber-Dochan has supervised six doctoral candidates to completion and currently supervise four more, two of whom are expected to complete within the next 12 months. Dr Czuber-Dochan has a longstanding commitment to supporting the academic progression of nurses, pharmacists, and allied health professionals, especially those from underrepresented backgrounds. In supporting my research, Dr Czuber-Dochan will provide: 1. Methodological expertise in systematic reviews, qualitative, quantitative and mixed methods research; 2. Structured supervision, including four-weekly meetings and support in managing a complex research programme; 3. Access to a well-established network of national and international researchers and clinicians with expertise in IBD and health equity; 4. Strategic input into the development of culturally tailored research methodologies and patient engagement strategies, particularly with ethnic minority populations including South Asians; 5. Guidance on the translation of findings into practice, with attention to addressing inequalities in care and outcomes; 6. Opportunities for dissemination via academic, policy, clinical, and community-facing platforms to maximise visibility and impact. This support will enable me to establish an independent research portfolio that not only advances pharmacist and allied health professional practice in IBD care and contributes meaningfully to the reduction of health disparities in underrepresented and ethnically diverse communities. Full Name Professor Vibhu Paudyal Organisation King's College London Email Address vibhu.paudyal@kcl.ac.uk Role Academic Supervisor Description of Support Professor Paudyal will be the primary contact point for realist review Provided and joint primary contact for the CPRD work. He will support application of inclusive research principles including the use of intersectional lens to understanding health inequalities and outcomes. He will also assist with PPIE strategies and input (including charity partners), development of study protocols, data collection, analysis and dissemination. Professor Paudyal has supervised 12 PhD students to completion, mainly in the roles of the Principal Supervisor and will offer valuable support around personal development, time management and team working.
